coredata.dc:title: N-doped Cdot/PtPd nanonetwork hybrid materials as highly efficient electrocatalysts for methanol oxidation and formic acid oxidation reactions
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Van-Toan
Tran, Quoc Chinh
Quang, Nguyen Duc
Nguyen, Ngoc-Anh
Bui, Van-Tien
Dao, Van-Duong
Choi, Ho-Suk
coredata.dc:description: The improvement of the catalytic performance for methanol and formic acid oxidation reactions remains a key issue for the development of a new generation of direct methanol and formic acid fuel cells. This study reports a simple approach, using selective chemical etching for the synthesis of unique nitrogen-doped carbon dot/Pt84Pd16 (N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16) nano-network structure at room temperature with excellent electrocatalytic properties. The obtained nano-network hybrid material exhibits significant enhancement of the electrocatalytic activity for the electro-oxidation reaction of both methanol and formic acid with current densities of 999.0 and 1919.5 mA/mgmetal, respectively, compared with the commercial ones of 751.32 and 806.02 mA/mgmetal, respectively. Furthermore, the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials exhibit excellent stability and hydrophilic dispersibility at room temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PtPd nanoalloy
Nano-network
Nitrogen-doped carbon dot
Methanol oxidation reaction
Formic acid oxidation reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberry hybrid materials
Preparation of the N-Cdot/PtPd nano-network hybrid materials
Catalytic activity of the nanohybrid materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by a National Research Foundation grant ( NRF-2017R1A2B2001911 ) funded by the Ministry of Science , BK21 , ICT and Future Planning, Republic of Korea. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of depicting the manufacturing procedure. TEM images of (a) N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberries (b) N-Cdot/Pt76Pd24 porous nano-raspberries, (c) N-Cdot/Pt81Pd19 hollow porous nano-raspberries, and (d) N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network. (e) Photographs of the etch solutions containing the etched nanoparticles at the bottom (dark dots) at different etch times. (f) Wide-range XRD patterns of various N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials at different etch times. The inset in (f) is an enlarged view of the (111) diffraction peak. The broad peak around 24° (*) represents the C-dots in the various N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials.

(a) TEM image and (b, c) HR-TEM images of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials at day 5 of etching. (d) HAADF-STEM images of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials. (e) EDX element mapping images for the (f) Pt atoms (red), (g) Pd atoms (green), and (h) C atoms (yellow). (i) HAADF-STEM and the (j) elemental distribution across a representative PtPd nanoalloy-nanowire in the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials using the STEM-EDS (energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) line scanning technique. The EDX scanning path is indicated by the arrow in (i). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) and (b) electrochemical surface areas (ECSAs) of the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts in an N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. CVs of the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts toward (c) MOR and (e) FAOR in 0.1 M KOH + 1.0 M CH3OH and 0.1 M HClO4 + 1.0 M HCOOH, respectively, under saturated N2 conditions at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Comparison of the oxidation current densities of the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts for (d) MOR and (f) FAOR at 0.09 V and maximum backward current density, respectively.

(a) Nyquist plots of EIS for c-Pt/C and N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts recorded in 1.0 M CH3OH and 0.1 M KOH at the potential of 0.4 V; the solid lines show fitted curves, (b) the equivalent circuit used for fitting the experimental data. (c) Stripping curves for CO oxidation on the different N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts with adsorption of CO in a 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (d) CO stripping curve exhibiting different onset potentials for the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts. The inset in Fig. 5d is the enlarged CO stripping curves at the peak potential region of the CO oxidation on different catalysts.

(a) LSV exhibiting different onset potentials for the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts toward MOR. (b) LSVs for the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid material catalysts indicating the positive peak potential shift. Chronoamperometric curves of the c-Pt/C (black line) and N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials in (c) 0.1 M KOH + 1.0 M CH3OH at −0.3 V and (d) 0.1 M HClO4 + 1.0 M HCOOH at 0.2 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 at 25 °C. The inset in Fig. 5c shows TEM image of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetwork after 3000 s chronoamperometric measurements of methanol oxidation reaction.
Binding energies and compositions of the nanoporous N-Cdot/PtPd nanoalloy catalysts measured from XPS and ICP-AES analyses.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Surface atomic ratio calculated from the XPS analyses using the peak areas normalized on the basis of sensitivity factors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Bulk atomic ratio obtained from the ICP-AES analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic properties of the catalysts for the methanol electro-oxidation reaction (MOR) and the formic acid electro-oxidation reaction (FAOR).

row.entry: Onset potential for MOR (V vs. RHE)

head.ce:title: N-doped Cdot/PtPd nanonetwork hybrid materials as highly efficient electrocatalysts for methanol oxidation and formic acid oxidation reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Chungnam National University, 220 Gung-Dong, Yuseong-Gu, Daejeon, 305-764, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Chungnam National University, 220 Gung-Dong, Yuseong-Gu, Daejeon, 305-764, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The improvement of the catalytic performance for methanol and formic acid oxidation reactions remains a key issue for the development of a new generation of direct methanol and formic acid fuel cells. This study reports a simple approach, using selective chemical etching for the synthesis of unique nitrogen-doped carbon dot/Pt84Pd16 (N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16) nano-network structure at room temperature with excellent electrocatalytic properties. The obtained nano-network hybrid material exhibits significant enhancement of the electrocatalytic activity for the electro-oxidation reaction of both methanol and formic acid with current densities of 999.0 and 1919.5 mA/mgmetal, respectively, compared with the commercial ones of 751.32 and 806.02 mA/mgmetal, respectively. Furthermore, the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials exhibit excellent stability and hydrophilic dispersibility at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) and direct formic acid fuel cells (DFAFCs) are receiving significant interest as critical components of portable and mobile device applications [1,2]. The most attractive features of DMFCs and DFAFCs are high efficiency, high energy density, low operating temperature, and low toxicity, as well as the ability to generate power without releasing gaseous toxic pollution from the by-products as in the case of coal-fired power plants [3,4]. However, the poor electrochemical activity and the high cost of the catalytic anode diminishes their potential for large-scale application [1–5].

Platinum-based catalysts are the most favorable catalysts for both methanol oxidation and formic acid oxidation in alkaline electrolytes [2–5]. However, a critical problem with Pt-based catalysts is that they are highly susceptible to be poisoned by surface-adsorbed reaction intermediates such as CO and/or unidentified species. In order to overcome the CO poisoning effect, to improve the electrocatalytic performance, and to reduce the use of Pt on metallic catalysts, Pt should be alloyed with other metals such as Ru, Pd, Co, Ni, Ti, V, and Au [4–13]. A number of methods have been proposed for designing network and hollow alloy nanoparticles that aim to provide more promising Pt-based catalysts such as Pt-Co, Pt-Ni, and Pt-Pd [10–13]. Among them, the network PtPd nanoalloy catalysts are promising electrocatalysts for fuel cells due to their electrochemical durability and efficient catalytic performance [13]. However, PtPd nanoalloy-network has been less investigated than that of hollow PtPd nanoalloy applied for DMFCs and DFAFCs in fuel cells. Therefore, the development of scalable catalysts that retain the electrocatalytic performance level remains a challenge in relation to current fuel cell technology.

In general, carbon nanomaterials, including carbon black, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and reduced graphene oxide or amorphous carbon, have been used as conducting supports for Pt-based catalysts in order to reduce agglomeration and to increase conductivity [10–13]. Note that the use of carbon-supported catalysts might cause a large loss of effective surface area associated with the detachment of nanocrystal catalysts from the carbon support. These drawbacks indicate the potential of a significant deterioration in performance of the fuel cells during operation [14,15]. Furthermore, the carbon supports tend to be deposited as layers on top of other layers, and they also tend to have many nanocrystals during the preparation of the working electrode, which leads to a large loss of effective surface area [16]. Nitrogen doped carbon supports can be a promising candidate to overcome these shortcomings of the carbon supports, because the presence of nitrogen functional groups on the surface of carbon support can improve their electronic structures and chemical reactivity, leading to strengthen the interaction between metal and carbon supports. It has been reported that the catalytic activity and durability toward MOR and ORR are improved [2,3].

Recently, carbon dots (C-dots) have received significant attention as a new type of carbon material with sizes below 10 nm, unique properties and potential applications in energy storage and conversion devices, optoelectronics, solar cells, biosciences, and biotechnologies [17–19]. They possess abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, resulting in favorable hydrophilicity. Cdots can function as both electron donors and electron acceptors [20–23]. In particular, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-Cdots) have recently been demonstrated to have potential to form stable and uniform catalyst films on glassy carbon electrodes and to improve the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalytic activity [19]. N-Cdots improve the electric conductivity and enhance the super-hydrophilicity and electrocatalytic activity. However, few investigations of the synthesis of N-Cdot/metal nanohybrid materials have been conducted to date [15].

In this study, a facile approach is presented for the preparation of high-quality N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials at room temperature. In the first step, N-Cdots are synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Then, the as-synthesized N-Cdots with an average size of 2 nm are used as seeds for the fabrication of N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry hybrid materials via a simple chemical co-reduction of both Pt and Pd precursors using formic acid at room temperature. In the second step, the morphology of the N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry hybrid materials is carefully controlled using a selective etching solution for the generation of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials. The N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network architecture provides abundant accessible sites, thus enabling tunable catalytic activity and excellent stability for both methanol oxidation reactions (MORs) and formic acid oxidation reactions (FAORs) relative to that of the commercial Pt catalyst.

Chloroplatinic acid hydrate (H2PtCl6.xH2O, 99.9% trace metals basis), potassium tetrachloropalladate (K2PdCl4, 99.99% trace metals basis), formic acid (HCOOH, 88% ACS reagent), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 97%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), ethanol (200 proof, USP, KOPTEC), citric acid (99.9%), and glycine (99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Note that these chemical reagents were used without further purification. The water (18.2 MΩ/cm) used in all experiments was prepared via passing through a Milli-Q® Integral Water Purification System (Merck Millipore, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberry hybrid materials
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen doped Cdots (N-Cdots) were synthesized and characterized as shown in the Supporting Information (Figs. S1–S5). The synthesis of the N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberry hybrid materials was performed as follows. First, 2 mg of N-Cdots was dispersed in 20 ml water and then transferred into a 50 ml vial. Second, 20 mg of H2PtCl6.xH2O and 20 mg of K2PdCl4 were added to the N-Cdot solution. Third, 1.5 mL of formic acid was added to the mixture, followed by ultrasonication for 5 min [21]. Fourth, the resulting homogeneous mixture was placed in a conventional oven and heated at 30 °C for 24 h (without stirring). The resulting black precipitate was collected after centrifuging the mixture at 7000 rpm and washing with ethanol at least five times. Finally, the product was dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C for 24 h.

PtPd nanoalloys shown in Figs. S6 and S7 were synthesized separately using the same procedure as described in Section 2.2, excluding the use of N-Cdot nanoparticles. Then, 5 mg of the as-synthesized PtPd nanoalloy catalysts were mixed with 20 mg of Super P carbon black, termed PtPd/C, for further electrochemical measurements [4–13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N-Cdot/PtPd nano-network hybrid materials
ce:section.ce:para: A galvanic replacement method was used to control the morphologies of the N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberry hybrid materials. In this step, the Pd atoms were etched from the N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberries by an aqueous solution containing a precise amount of FeCl3 and HCl at an ambient temperature. The morphology of the porous N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberries was further controlled through the time, until, an N-Cdot/PtPd nano-network hybrid material was generated. Briefly, the homogeneous solution contains 30 mg FeCl3, 300 mg KBr, 18 ml HCl (37%), and 7 ml water was prepared in a 20 ml glass vial. Next, 5 mg of N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberries was added to 50 ml of the as-prepared solution. The leaching reaction was performed at room temperature without stirring. After 5 days, the black precipitate was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for collection, washed three times with distilled water and twice with ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C for 24 h for further use.

N-Cdots were successfully synthesized with spherical shape and size ranging from 1.5 to 2.5 nm in diameter as shown in Figs. S1–S5 of “Supporting information“ part. The N-Cdots were used to synthesize N-Cdot/PtPd nano-raspberry as shown in Fig. 1 a. Specifically, the schematic illustration of the manufacturing procedure and XRD patterns of various N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials are shown in Fig. 1. Fig. 1a and Figs. S8a–i exhibit the as-prepared products, which appear similar to a bunch of grapes composed of small nano-raspberries with an average diameter of approximately 186 ± 0.5 nm. The Pd and carbon atoms were comparatively homogeneously distributed inside the particles, whereas the Pt atoms were relatively concentrated at the edges of the particles (Figs. S8f–i). According to the ICP analyses, the stoichiometric ratio between the Pt and Pd atoms was 53:47 (N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberries).

In addition, Fig. 1e presents a photograph of etching solutions containing the etched nanoparticles at the bottom of bottle (dark dots) at different etch times. The etch solution gradually changed from a bright yellow to a light brown color as increasing etching time from 0 to 5 days. The changed color is due to the increase in the concentration of Pd2+ ions, resulting from leaching Pd atoms from the N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry during the etching process. The UV–Vis measurements were performed for the remaining etch solutions at different times to gain the information of leaching of the Pd atoms (Fig. S12). The absorption intensity of the band near 300 nm was slightly increased from 0 to 5 days, respectively. This band was attributed to the formation of the Pd2+ ions in the etch solution.

The XRD measurements were, further, conducted to highlight the crystal structure of the various N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials at different etch time. Fig. 1f illustrates that the diffraction peak at approximately 24° indicates the (002) lattice of the carbon of N-Cdots in the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials [17]. Note that the initial nano-raspberries were gradually eroded into the nano-network and the bulk composition changed from Pt53Pd47 to Pt76Pd24, then to Pt81Pd19, and eventually to Pt84Pd16. Fig. 1f displays the diffraction peaks at approximately 40.25°, 46.80°, 68.20°, and 81.70°, which were characteristics of the face-centered cubic (fcc) crystalline of the N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry at day 0, corresponding to the lattice planes of (111), (200), (220), and (311), respectively [24]. The inset in Fig. 1f presents an enlarged view of the diffraction peak at the (111) lattice plane of four different samples at day 0, 1, 3, and 5 of etching. The diffraction peak shifted slightly toward the lower angle compared with the as-prepared N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry when the etch time increased from 0 to 5 days. This result implies that the nanostructures changed to Pt-rich nanoalloys, which is in good accordance with the ICP results. In addition, Table 1 and Figs. S13a–c describe the binding energies of the electrons, a shift in the XPS peak and relative amounts of different oxidation states for the Pt 4f7/2 and Pd 3d5/2 regions. The Pt: Pd atomic ratio on the surface of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials calculated from the XPS spectra was 91:9 (see Table 1), which reveals a significant difference with the bulk atomic ratio determined by ICP (84:16). The difference between the atomic ratio on the surface and the bulk atomic ratio indicates that the Pt84Pd16 nanoalloy structure had a remarkably Pt-enriched surface.

To highlight comprehensively the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetwork structure, we also conducted the HR-TEM imaging and EDX element mapping. A large number of PtPd nanowires are interconnected to form a network architecture with a quasi-spherical shape (Fig. 2 a–c and S11a-b). Fig. 2c reveals a lattice spacing of 0.237 nm and 0.195 nm that can be assigned to the (111) and (200) planes of the PtPd nanoalloy, respectively [12]. In addition, Fig. 2g–h depict that the Pd and C atoms were homogeneously distributed in the entire nanonetwork-like spheres, whereas the Pt atoms tended to concentrate at the edges of the nanonetwork-like spheres (Fig. 2f). The STEM/EDS image depicts the distribution of the Pt, Pd, and C atoms in an individual nanowire (Fig. 2i–j), which illustrates that the Pt atoms were more abundant than the Pd and C atoms in the outer region and the Pd atoms were distributed uniformly over the C atoms, forming the core (Fig. 2j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of the nanohybrid materials

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a presents the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of all catalysts and a commercial Pt/C catalyst (c-Pt/C). From the changes associated with the desorption of hydrogen, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of each catalyst was derived and normalized against the metal loading. The ECSAs were estimated to be 89.9, 80.3, 76.3, 58.0, and 53.3 m2/gmetal for the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network, porous hollow N-Cdot/Pt81Pd19 nano-raspberry, porous N-Cdot/Pt76P24 nano-raspberry, N-Cdot/Pt53P47 nano-raspberry, and c-Pt/C, respectively (Fig. 3a–b and Table S2). Clearly, the ECSA of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetworks was much higher than those of others. This result reflects that the highly branched structure of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials provides a relatively larger surface area. Furthermore, Fig. 3a also illustrates that the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials were more electrochemically susceptible, indicating that the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network possessed more considerably accessible sites than the other materials.

The MOR performance of the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials was evaluated and compared with the PtPd/C and c-Pt/C electrocatalysts, as depicted in Fig. 3c and S14a. The peak current density of the N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry is about 768.95 mA/mgmetal (Fig. S14a, 3c-d and Table S2), which was quite higher than those of PtPd/C (700.01 mA/mgmetal) and c-Pt/C (751.32 mA/mgmetal), respectively. It results from the role of the N-Cdots in the N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nanohybrid material, leading to optimize the electron transferability of the reactants to the electrocatalyst [2,3]. Fig. 3c–d presents the current density of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetworks materials in the positive direction sweep, which was found to be 1.32 and 1.30 times higher than those of the c-Pt/C and N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry, respectively. Therefore, this result is attributed to the unique nano-network hybrid structure of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 material.

The electrocatalytic activities toward formic acid electro-oxidation of all nanohybrid materials were also carefully investigated, as depicted in Fig. 3e–f. The CV profiles (Fig. 3e) exhibit an identical trend for all catalysts: the FAOR occurred via the triple-pathway mechanism. The prominent peaks (peak III) range from 0.0 to 0.90 V vs RHE in the negative sweep, corresponding to the formic acid oxidation via the formate anion pathways [25,26]. The oxidation reaction involves the removal of hydrogen to generate formate anions, and then these anions are oxidized to form CO2. The CV curves are split into two peaks during the positive scan (Fig. 3e), these peaks correspond to peak I and peak II at approximately 0.50 and 0.93 V vs RHE, respectively. Peak I relates to the direct oxidation pathways of FAOR via the dehydrogenation mechanism (HCOOH → CO2 + 2H+ + 2e−), involving the removal of two hydrogen atoms to form CO2 [27]. Peak II is an indirect oxidation pathway of the dehydration reaction of HCOOH [27,28]. Clearly, the current densities of peak III are remarkably higher than those of peak I and peak II. Thus, the oxidation of the formic acid occurs via the formate anion pathways on the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials. Fig. 3e–f depict an increase in the backward peak current densities in the following magnitude: c-Pt/C < N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 < N-Cdot/Pt76Pd24 < N-Cdot/Pt81Pd19 < N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16. In particular, the backward current density of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network was 2.05 times higher than that of the N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry (Fig. 3f). This enhancement can be attributed to the modification of the electronic structure in the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanohybrid material and the unique nanonetwork structure.

The electrochemical impedance spectra were further investigated for the kinetics of methanol oxidation and formic acid oxidation of each catalyst. The Nyquist plots of the different N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials, PtPd/C, and c-Pt/C catalysts are shown in Fig. 4a and S15a. The resultant EIS data were fitted by an electric equivalent circuit in Fig. 4b and S15b. In the electric equivalent circuit, the Rs is the solution resistance, constant phase element (CPE) consists of CPE1-T and CPE1-P accounting for frequency dispersion in double layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance, respectively. Rct1 is charge transfer resistance. Table S1 shows a decrease in the Rct1 in the following amplitude: PtPd/C (1071.0 Ω) > N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 (523.5 Ω) > N-Cdot/Pt76Pd24 (355.0 Ω) > N-Cdot/Pt81Pd19 (151.5 Ω) > c-Pt/C (138.4 Ω) > N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 (92.9 Ω). Actually, the Rct1 values of N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry and N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network were remarkably lower than that of PtPd/C (2.04 times) and N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry (5.63 times), respectively. These results suggest that the enhanced activities of N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry and N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network is due to the acceleration of the electron transfer [29–32].

In addition, the ratio of the cathodic peak to the anodic peak current density (If/Ib) is an important index for evaluating the catalytic tolerance toward the poisoning species, caused by the carbonaceous intermediates adsorbed on the Pt surface [29–32]. A higher If/Ib ratio indicates that the electrocatalysts oxidize methanol more effectively and generate less poisoning species during the MOR operation. As described in Table 2 , the If/Ib ratio of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network (6.84) is the highest. Furthermore, the tolerance of the COads poisoning of each catalyst was investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 c–d and S15c-d. Obviously, the CO tolerance of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 is the highest among the other catalysts (Fig. 4c), which reveals the consistency with the result of the above MOR measurements. However, the onset potential and the CO peak potential on the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network catalyst exhibited a negative shift compared to the others (Fig. 4d), indicating that the presence of N-Cdot and unique network structure of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network facilitate the electrocatalytic oxidation of the COads species.

In Fig. 5 a, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) exhibits a change in the onset and peak potentials for the methanol electro-oxidation. The onset potential of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network materials was 416 mV vs RHE, whereas the c-Pt/C and N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry exhibited the onset at 554 and 548 mV vs RHE, respectively. This indicates that the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network material displays much better activity than those of the c-Pt/C and N-Cdot/Pt53Pd47 nano-raspberry. Moreover, Fig. 5b depicts the peak potentials of all N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials moving toward positive potentials and the increasement of the current densities with increasing the etch time from 0 to 5 days. This result is similar to the observation proposed by Kakade et al. for the methanol electro-oxidation of bimetallic PdPt nanocrystals [6]. In contrast, the N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials could well control the Pt: Pd ratio through a selective etching process, while the N-Cdot species were integrated into the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetwork structure. It is valuable, therefore, to understand the greater improvement of MOR performance of the developed nano-network hybrid materials due to two primary factors. First, the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetworks could provide more active sites. Second, the presence of the N-Cdot in the Pt84Pd16 nanonetworks will accelerate the electron movement.

The durability of all catalysts was examined via chronoamperometric measurements at 0.65 V vs RHE in a 0.1 M KOH +1.0 M CH3OH solution for 3000 s and at 0.43 V vs RHE in a 0.1 M HClO4 + 1.0 M HCOOH solution for 1200 s for the MOR and FAOR, respectively. Fig. 5c and d presents the typical current densities plotted against time for all catalysts for the MOR and FAOR, respectively. The c-Pt/C benchmark catalyst revealed a rapid current decay and maintained 30% of its original current density, whereas the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanoparticles retained 72% of their original current density, even after 3000 s (Fig. 5c). The TEM image of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network also reveals that, even after 3000 s chronoamperometric measurements of oxidation reaction, there was no significant change in the morphology of the hybrid nanostructures, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5c. Hence, the excellent electrocatalytic activity and stability of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network make them suitable for direct methanol fuel cell application. In case of FAOR, N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 exhibited a significantly higher current density than that of other N-Cdot/PtPd nanohybrid materials and c-Pt/C during the entire time period (Fig. 5d and S16). Furthermore, N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 exhibited significantly higher limiting current density than that of the others, indicating excellent catalytic activity and stability.This result confirms that the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 catalyst has higher electrocatalytic activity and durability than the other materials. These improvements of the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetwork can be supposed to be due to both the Pt-enriched surface of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetwork and the optimization of the ratio of Pt to Pd atoms.

We have successfully developed a facile method for the preparation of N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network hybrid materials through combining N-Cdot nanoparticles used as seeds and a selective etching agent. The method can control the morphology and Pt/Pd atomic ratio in order to gradually generate the nano-network architecture. Hence, this method is an important addition to satisfy recently realized noble metal nanonetworks. In addition, the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network, consisting of hierarchically interconnected nanowires, was found to have a unique structure generated through a selective etching solution. The good synergistic effects between the network architecture and the presence of N-Cdots in the N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nano-network provide a unique approach to systematically tune both the active surface areas and the electron transfer of the catalyst, therefore, the optimal electrocatalytic activity can be achieved. The N-Cdot/Pt84Pd16 nanonetworks exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity for both methanol oxidation and formic acid oxidation reactions with current densities of 999.0 and 1919.5 mA/mgmetal, respectively. Thus, the proposed strategy provides a new approach that fulfills various requirements for the development of high performance Pt-based nanohybrid materials for fuel cell applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a National Research Foundation grant (NRF-2017R1A2B2001911) funded by the Ministry of Science, BK21, ICT and Future Planning, Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.07.049.
